title: LI: Sergeant-Major Archdale
html:
LI

Sergeant-Major Archdale

Harry Fairfield was a captain in his county militia. It was right that the House of Fairfield should be represented in that corps. Charlie, who was of an easy compliant temper, would have taken the commission and the light duties, if that dignity had been put upon him. But Harry chose it. It extended his acquaintance, added to his opportunities of selling his horses, and opened some houses, small and great, to him, in a neighbourly fashion, when making his circuits to fair and market. He knew something of games, too, and was shrewd at whist and draughts, and held a sure cue at billiards. On the whole, his commission turned him in something in the course of a year.

It was upon some regimental business that Sergeant-Major Archdale was awaiting his return at Wyvern.

Harry Fairfield, as it happened, was thinking of the Sergeant as he rode into the yard in gloomy rumination.

вАЬWell, Archdale, whatвАЩs the news?вАЭ said he, as he dismounted.

The news was not a great deal. After he had heard it Harry paused for a time, and said heпїњвАФ

вАЬQuite well, Archdale, I hope?вАЭ

вАЬWell, sir, I thank you.вАЭ

Again Harry paused.

вАЬHow did you come, Archdale?вАЭ

вАЬWalked, sir.вАЭ

вАЬWalked, oh! very well.вАЭ

Here was another pause.

вАЬArchdale, you must go in. Here, Clinton, get some luncheon for Sergeant-Major Archdale. A drink of beer and a mouthful wonвАЩt do you no harm; and, Archdale, before you go let me know; I may have a word, and IвАЩll say it walking down the avenue. Get Mr.¬†Archdale some luncheon, Clinton, and some sherry.вАЭ

вАЬI thank you, sir,вАЭ said the Sergeant-Major. вАЬвАКвАЩTis more like a supper for me; IвАЩve had my dinner, sir, some time.вАЭ

And with a stiff military step the Sergeant followed Clinton into the house.

The Sergeant-Major was above the middle size, and stout of body, which made him look shorter. His hair was closely cut, and of a pale blue iron gray. His face was rather pale, and smooth as marble; full and long, with a blue chin, and a sort of light upon his fixed lineaments, not exactly a smile, but a light that was treacherous and cruel. For the rest his military coat, which was of the old-fashioned cut, and his shako, with all the brasses belonging to them, and his Wellington boots, were natty and brilliant, and altogether unexceptionable, and a more perfectly respectable-looking man you could not have found in his rank of life in the country.

Without a word, with a creak in his boots, he marched slowly in, with inflexible countenance, after Clinton.

The Squire met Harry in the hall.

вАЬHollo! itвАЩs a week a most since I set eyes on yeпїњвАФyeвАЩll look out some other place for that mad filly ye bought of Jim Hardress: sheвАЩs broke a boyвАЩs arm this morning in the stable; IвАЩll not look after him, I promise ye; вАЩtis your affair, mind, and you better look sharp, and delay may cost ye money. YeвАЩre over clever. The devil owes ye a cake this many a day, and heвАЩs a busy bishop, and heвАЩll pay ye a loaf yet, I promise you. She shanвАЩt be kicking my menпїњвАФand she bites the manger besides. Get her away, mind, or, by my soul, IвАЩll sell her for the damage.вАЭ

So old Squire Harry stalked on, and the last scion of his stock grinned after him, sulkily, and snarled something between his teeth, so soon as he was quite out of hearing.

вАЬWhoвАЩs armвАЩs broke, Dick, or is it all a dпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњd lie oвАЩ the GovernorвАЩs?вАЭ inquired Harry of a servant who happened to be passing at that moment.

вАЬWell, yes, sir, Jim SladeвАЩs arm was broke in the stable. вАЩTwas a kick, sir.вАЭ

вАЬWhat kicked him?вАЭ

вАЬThe new horse that came in on Thursday, sir.вАЭ

вАЬMare, ye mean. Why that thingвАЩs a regвАЩlar lamb; she never kicked no one. A child might play wiвАЩ her. More like вАЩtwas the Governor kicked him. And what did he do wiвАЩ his arm?вАЭ

вАЬThe doctor, down in the town, set it, and bound it up wiвАЩ splints, sir.вАЭ

вАЬWell, I didnвАЩt tell him, mind thatпїњвАФI wasnвАЩt here, ye knowпїњвАФgood-natured of the doctor, IвАЩll not deny, but he shanвАЩt be sending in no bills to me. And howвАЩs Jim sinceпїњвАФgettinвАЩ on nicely, IвАЩll swear.вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt know, sir; I didnвАЩt see him since.вАЭ

вАЬHoot! then itвАЩs all right, I warrant ye, and ye can tell old Slade, if he likes it, IвАЩll get him a bit of a writinвАЩ to the hospital for Jim; but it wonвАЩt be nothinвАЩпїњвАФnot a bit.вАЭ

And with this economical arrangement, Harry dismissed the subject for the present, and took his stand upon the hall-door steps, and smoked his pipe, awaiting the close of Sergeant-Major ArchdaleвАЩs repast.

The long shadows and lights of golden sunset faded before the guest appeared, and twilight and the moths were abroad.

Almost as the servant informed Harry Fairfield that Mr.¬†Archdale was coming round to the hall-door to receive his commands, the Sergeant-Major appeared in front of the house, and Harry Fairfield stepped down to the court and was received by the militiaman with a military salute.

вАЬIвАЩll walk a bit wiвАЩ you, Archdale; I want a word about another matterпїњвАФnot regimental business. WeвАЩll walk down towards the gate.вАЭ

Stiffly and silently the Sergeant-Major marched beside the smoking gentleman, who having got a little way from the house, knocked the ashes out of his pipe, and dropped it into his pocket.

вАЬThat militia sogerinвАЩ is beggarly pay for a man like you, Archdale; and IвАЩll want a clever fellow, by-and-byпїњвАФfor when the Squire goes off the hooks, and that canвАЩt be a long way offпїњвАФIвАЩll have a deal oвАЩ trouble lookinвАЩ after things; for thereвАЩs a young chap to succeed, and a plaguy long minority вАЩtwill be, and one way or another the trouble will fall to my share, beinвАЩ uncle, ye see, to the little fellow. Am I making it plain what I mean?вАЭ

вАЬQuite plain, sir,вАЭ said the cold voice of the Sergeant-Major.

вАЬWell, thereвАЩs the property down at Warhampton, a devilish wide stretch oвАЩ land for the rental. ThereвАЩs good shootinвАЩ there, and two keepers, but I doubt they makes away wiвАЩ the game, and they want lookinвАЩ after; and thereвАЩs the old park oвАЩ WarhamptonпїњвАФye know that part oвАЩ the country?вАЭ

вАЬYes, sir, well.вАЭ

вАЬI know you do. Well, it should turn in a good penny more than the Governor gets. I canвАЩt bring it home to them, but I know what I think. Where the horse lies down, the hair will be founвАЩ, and I doubt the park-bookвАЩs doctored. ThereвАЩll be a sort oвАЩ steward wanted there, dвАЩye see. DвАЩye know Noulton farm?вАЭ

вАЬYes, sir.вАЭ

вАЬWell, itвАЩs a nice thing, a snug house, and as many acres as youвАЩd want to begin wiвАЩ; the tenantвАЩs going after harvestпїњвАФyouвАЩd be the very man forвАЩt, and IвАЩll tell them IвАЩll do all I can to serve my nephew, but I must live myself too. IвАЩve nout but my time and my wits to turn a penny by, and if I try to manage for him IвАЩll want the best help I can get, dвАЩye see? and youвАЩre the man I want; IвАЩve got no end oвАЩ a character oвАЩ ye, for honesty and steadiness and the like; and yeвАЩre a fellow can use his eyes, and hold his tongue; and yeвАЩd have the farm and the houseпїњвАФye know themпїњвАФrent free; and the grazing of three cows on the common, and itвАЩs none oвАЩ your overstocked, bare commons, but as sweet a bit oвАЩ grass as yeвАЩd find in the kingdom; and ye shall aвАЩ fifty pounds a year beside; and the farmвАЩs nigh forty acres, and itвАЩs worth close on a hundred more. AndпїњвАФif ye do all we want well, and IвАЩm sure you willпїњвАФIвАЩll never lose sight oвАЩ ye while grass grows and you and me lives.вАЭ

вАЬI thank you, sir,вАЭ said the cold, clear voice of Archdale.

вАЬAnd thereвАЩs a little bit of a secretпїњвАФI wouldnвАЩt tell anotherпїњвАФabout myself, Archdale. IвАЩll tell you, though,вАЭ said Harry, lowering his voice.

вАЬYes, sir,вАЭ said Archdale, in the same cold stern way, which irritated Harry.

вАЬWell, IвАЩm not talking, mind, to Sergeant-Major Archdale, if you like the other thing, at Noulton, best.вАЭ

вАЬNoulton best, sir, certainly; thank you.вАЭ

вАЬBut to Mr.¬†Archdale of Noulton, and steward of Warhampton, mind ye, and вАЩtwill be settled next harvest.вАЭ

вАЬI thank you, sir.вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt walk so quick, weвАЩre gettinвАЩ over the ground too fast. Well, thereвАЩs a thing youвАЩll have to keep dark for me.вАЭ

вАЬYouвАЩll find me confidential, sir; my superior officers did.вАЭ

вАЬI know that wellпїњвАФI know you, Archdale, and that is why I chose you out oвАЩ a thousand, and itвАЩs a confidential fellowпїњвАФdпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњd confidentialпїњвАФI want, for the countryвАЩs all one as the town for talk, and tongues will keep goinвАЩ like the bells on a sheepwalk, and thereвАЩs many a bit oвАЩ nonsense, thatвАЩs no great odds when allвАЩs told, that a chap wouldnвАЩt like to have made the laugh or the talk oвАЩ the country side.вАЭ

вАЬYes, sir,вАЭ said the inflexible Sergeant-Major.

вАЬYou held the same rank in the line, Sergeant-Major, didnвАЩt you?вАЭ

вАЬYes, sir,вАЭ said the Sergeant-Major, and saluted from habit.

вАЬI thought so, and that says a deal for you, Mr.¬†Archdale; and I remember one of your papers says you were the youngest sergeant ever made in your regiment?вАЭ

вАЬYes, sir.вАЭ

вАЬWell, that says a lot too, and a very responsible office that is. Egad, from all I вАЩa seen, IвАЩd say the sergeants has more to do with the state of a regiment than all the other officers, commissioned or noncommissioned, put together.вАЭ

вАЬThereвАЩs a good deal depends on вАЩem, sir.вАЭ

вАЬYou keep to yourself, Archdale; thatвАЩs the way to rise.вАЭ

вАЬI was a man of few acquaintances, sir, and confidential with my superior officers, and few words, but I meant вАЩem, sir, and made the men do their duty.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs the man for my money,вАЭ said Harry. вАЬWill ye be ready for Noulton Farm by the middle oвАЩ next month?вАЭ

вАЬYes, sir, I expect.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩll settle that for ye, then, and the pay and the commonage. IвАЩll settle that wiвАЩ my father tomorrow, and weвАЩll get the writings drawn.вАЭ

вАЬI thank you, sir.вАЭ

вАЬAnd, wait a bit. I told you,вАЭ said Harry, perhaps a very little embarrassed, вАЬthereвАЩs another little thing you must manage for me.вАЭ

вАЬYes, sir.вАЭ

He almost wished Mr.¬†Archdale to ask questions and raise difficulties. This icy surface, beneath which he saw nothing, began to embarrass him.

вАЬEvery fellowвАЩs a fool once or twice in his life, you know, Archdale; and thatвАЩs the way rogues makes money, and honest chaps is soldпїњвАФ

вАШNo fools at the fair,
No sale for bad ware,вАЩ

you know?вАЭ

He looked for sympathy in the face of the Sergeant-Major, but he found there neither sympathy nor ridicule, but a serene, dignified, supercilious composure.

вАЬWell, IвАЩm not married, and moreвАЩs the pity,вАЭ he said, affecting a kind of jocularity, uneasily; вАЬbut among вАЩem theyвАЩve made me a present of a brat they calls my son, and I must just put him to nurse and provide for him, I do suppose; and keep all quiet, and yeвАЩll look out some decent poor body that lives lonely and wonвАЩt ask no questions nor give no trouble, but be content wiвАЩ a trifle, and IвАЩll giвАЩeвАЩt to you every quarter for her, and sheвАЩll never hear my name, mind, nor be the wiser who owns it or where it came from. IвАЩd rayther she thought вАЩtwas a poor bodyвАЩsпїњвАФif they think a fellowвАЩs well-to-do it makes вАЩem unreasonable, and thatвАЩs the reason I pitched on you, Archdale, because yeвАЩre a man oвАЩ sense, and wonвАЩt be talkinвАЩ like the pratinвАЩ fools thatвАЩs goinвАЩпїњвАФand is it settled? is it a bargain?вАЭ

вАЬYes, sir, I thank you, quite,вАЭ said Archdale.

вАЬWell, then, ye shall hear from me by the end oвАЩ the week, and not a word, mindпїњвАФtill allвАЩs signed and sealedпїњвАФabout Noulton Farm, and about tвАЩother thingпїњвАФnever. The stars is cominвАЩ out bright, and the sunset did ye mind; weвАЩll вАЩa frost tonight; itвАЩs come dark very sudden; sharp air.вАЭ

He paused, but the noncommissioned officer did not venture a kindred remark, even an acquiescence in these meteorological speculations.

вАЬAnd I heard the other day you made an organ for Mr.¬†Arden. Is it true?вАЭ said Harry, suddenly.

вАЬJust a small thing, three stops, sirпїњвАФdiapason, principal, and dulciana.вАЭ

вАЬWell, I donвАЩt know nothing myself about such gear, except to hear the old organ oвАЩ Wyvern oвАЩ Sundays. But itвАЩs clever oвАЩ you. How did ye learn?вАЭ

вАЬвАКвАЩPrenticed, sir, two years to an organ builder in WestminsterпїњвАФMr.¬†LomasпїњвАФand he died, and I was put to the army,вАЭ said Archdale.

вАЬWell, I may give ye a lift that way, too. They were talkinвАЩ of an organ for Warhampton Church. WeвАЩll see. IвАЩll not forget.вАЭ

вАЬI thank you, sir,вАЭ repeated Archdale. вАЬAny more commands for me, sir?вАЭ

Mr.¬†Archdale stood stiffly at the gate, drawn up, as it were, at right angles to Harry Fairfield.

вАЬNo, nothing, Archdale. IвАЩm glad the thing suits you, and it may lie in my way yet to make them better than you think for. Good night, Archdale; good night, Sergeant-Major.вАЭ

вАЬGood night, sir.вАЭ

And Archdale wheeled to his left, and with his back toward the village of Wyvern, marched away at so stiff and regular a quick march that you could have fancied the accompaniment of the drums and fifes.

Harry stood at the iron gate, one half of which was open, and he kicked a stone listlessly into the road, and, leaning on the old iron arabesques, he looked long after that portly figure receding in distance and melting in twilight.

вАЬNightвАЩs the mother oвАЩ thought, IвАЩve heard say,вАЭ said Harry, rousing himself, and swinging the great valve into its place with a clang. вАЬBut thought wonвАЩt do to dine on. Hollo! Gate! gate! Jorrocks, anyone,вАЭ he shouted. вАЬLock the gate, some of you, and make all sure for the night.вАЭ

And with these orders to Jorrocks, he marched back under the ancestral trees to the old hall of Wyvern. Who was to keep the hearth of the Fairfields aglow? The light of the old SquireвАЩs life was flaring low in the socket, a tiny taper was just lighted in darksome Carwell, and Harry FairfieldпїњвАФwas he ever to take his turn and illuminate the Wyvern world?
